coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent carbon dots based sensing system for detection of enrofloxacin in water solutions
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Xingjia
Zhang, Lizhi
Wang, Zuowei
Sun, Yuting
Liu, Qingshi
Dong, Wei
Hao, Aijun
coredata.dc:description: Enrofloxacin (ENR) is one of the environmental pollutants need to remove in many wastewater treatment processes. Traditional methods for measuring ENR are often complex and time-consuming. Due to their low cost and high efficiency, fluorescent carbon dots can be used for detecting many pharmaceuticals. In this contribution, nitrogen doped fluorescent carbon dots (N-CDs) were firstly synthesized with a fluorescence quantum yield of 20.5%. The N-CDs can emit strong blue fluorescence when excited at 368 nm and there exist a large amount of carboxyl, hydroxyl and amine groups on their surfaces. In addition, the fluorescence of N-CDs could be quenched in the presence of Cu2+, which could be gradually restored upon adding ENR. Thereby, a rapid and sensitive fluorescent sensing strategy based on the fluorescence recovery of the N-CDs-Cu2+ system was designed for selective detection of ENR. The possible sensing mechanism was also proposed in terms of the results of resonance Rayleigh scattering, UV–vis absorption and Fourier transform infrared (FITR) spectra. Under the optimal condition, a good linear relationship was obtained for ENR determination with concentrations ranging from 1.0 to 15.0 μg·mL−1 and the detection limit of 0.16 μg·mL−1 was achieved. Finally the proposed sensing system was applied for the detection of ENR in real water samples with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent carbon dots
Enrofloxacin
Fluorescence probe

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cu2+ quenching and ENR recovery procedure
Collection and analysis of water samples
Fluorescence quenching and recovery of N-CDs
Optimization of experimental conditions for ENR sensing
Determination of ENR in real water samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–vis absorption spectrum of N-CDs (a), fluorescence excitation (b) and emission (c) spectra of N-CDs. (B) PL spectra of N-CDs with different excitation wavelengths. (C) TEM image of N-CDs. Inset: HRTEM image of N-CDs. (D) The particle size distribution histograms of N-CDs.
XRD pattern (A) and FT-IR spectrum (B) of N-CDs.
XPS survey spectrum of the N-CDs (A) and the corresponding C1s (B), N1 s (C), and O1s (D) spectra.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs (a), N-CD-Cu2+ (b), and N-CDs-Cu2++ENR (c). Concentrations of N-CDs, ENR, and Cu2+ were 600, 15, and 500 μg·mL−1, respectively. (B) PL spectra of N-CDs after adding different concentrations of Cu2+. Concentrations of Cu2+ (from a to m) are 0.0, 10, 30, 50, 70, 100, 300, 500, 700, 1000, 1100 and 1300 μg·mL−1, respectively. Inset: relationship between F/F0 and Cu2+ concentration changing from 0.0 to 1300 μg·mL−1.

(A) PL spectra of N-CDs upon adding various amounts of ENR. (B) The scattering spectra of N-CDs after adding different amounts of Cu2+. (C) UV–vis absorption spectra of the N-CDs before and after addition of Cu2+. (D) FT-IR spectra of ENR (a) and ENR–Cu2+ (b).

(A) Effect of pH on ΔF. The concentrations of the N-CDs, ENR, and Cu2+ were 600, 15, and 500 μg·mL−1, respectively; (B) Effect of the N-CDs concentration on ΔF. The concentrations of ENR and Cu2+ were 15 and 500 μg·mL−1, respectively; (C) Effect of incubation time on ΔF. The concentrations of the N-CDs, ENR, and Cu2+ were 600, 15, and 500 μg·mL−1, respectively.

PL spectra of the N-CDs-Cu2+ system after adding different concentrations of ENR. Concentrations of ENR (from a to h) are 0.0, 1.0, 3.0, 5.0, 7.0, 10, 13, and 15 μg·mL−1, respectively. Inset: linear relationship between ΔF and ENR concentration in the range of 1.0 to 15 μg·mL−1.

Effects of potential interfering substances on the FL enhancement of the N-CDs-Cu2+ system caused by 2 μg·mL−1 ENR. Concentrations: Na+/Cl− and K+ (30 mmol·L−1 for each); Mg2+ and Ca2+ (10 mmol·L−1 for each); Hg2+, Pb2+, Fe2+, and Fe3+ (0.1 mmol·L−1 for each); CO3 2−, HCO3 −, SO4 2−, and NO3 − (1 mmol·L−1 for each); urea, glucose, and uric acid (1 mmol·L−1 for each); ascorbic acid (0.5 mmol·L−1); LMFH (Lomefloxacin hydrochloride) and Ciprofloxacin (0.01 mmol·L−1 for each); no interfering: The mixture of N-CDs-Cu2+ in the presence of ENR without interfering substances.
Schematic illustration of ENR sensing process using N-CDs fluorescent probe.
Determination of ENR in real water samples (n = 3).
Comparison of the proposed method with previously reported methods for the determination of ENR.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent carbon dots based sensing system for detection of enrofloxacin in water solutions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, Shenyang 110036, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Shenyang Medical College, Shenyang 110034, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Pharmacy, Liaoning University, Shenyang 110036, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Enrofloxacin (ENR) is one of the environmental pollutants need to remove in many wastewater treatment processes. Traditional methods for measuring ENR are often complex and time-consuming. Due to their low cost and high efficiency, fluorescent carbon dots can be used for detecting many pharmaceuticals. In this contribution, nitrogen doped fluorescent carbon dots (N-CDs) were firstly synthesized with a fluorescence quantum yield of 20.5%. The N-CDs can emit strong blue fluorescence when excited at 368 nm and there exist a large amount of carboxyl, hydroxyl and amine groups on their surfaces. In addition, the fluorescence of N-CDs could be quenched in the presence of Cu2+, which could be gradually restored upon adding ENR. Thereby, a rapid and sensitive fluorescent sensing strategy based on the fluorescence recovery of the N-CDs-Cu2+ system was designed for selective detection of ENR. The possible sensing mechanism was also proposed in terms of the results of resonance Rayleigh scattering, UV–vis absorption and Fourier transform infrared (FITR) spectra. Under the optimal condition, a good linear relationship was obtained for ENR determination with concentrations ranging from 1.0 to 15.0 μg·mL−1 and the detection limit of 0.16 μg·mL−1 was achieved. Finally the proposed sensing system was applied for the detection of ENR in real water samples with satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:para: Since the discovery of penicillin in 1928, antibiotics have been playing an important role in people's lives. In dairy farms, antibiotics are widely used to treat various diseases including bacterial infections such as Mastitis and Cystitis. However, very little of the applied antibiotics is absorbed while the majority is excreted without modification. The left antibiotics can cause serious environmental problems, and also lead to drug-resistance and serious effects on human health, such as mild nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, insomnia, and severe death [1–3]. Therefore, in order to avoid a series of problems, much attention has been paid to monitor the distribution of these antibiotics in biological and environmental samples [4–6].

Enrofloxacin is one of the synthetic fluoroquinolones which is mainly used in veterinary medicine [3]. As we know, ENR cannot be completely absorbed by animal body, when ENR was administered subcutaneously to animals. For example, the s.c. bioavailability of ENR in Thai swamp buffaloes was 52.36 ± 4.24% and 72.12 ± 5.39% at doses of 5.0 and 7.5 mg/kg, respectively. And ENR was detectable in urine and feces up to 24 h [7]. Some of them will enter water and soil systems through waste and pollute the environment. Thus, it is very important to develop a facile, simple method to detect ENR concentration in water solution. Up to now, some techniques, such as HPLC [8–10], MS [11,12], spectrophotometry [13], fluorescence method [14,15], electrophoretic method [16,17] have been developed for ENR analysis. Although these methods are sensitive and accurate, they also have some shortages, such as relaying on expensive instruments, time-consuming, complex operation procedure [15,18,19]. Therefore, it is necessary to develop novel not only sensitive and selective, but also fast and simple ENR analysis methods.

Fluorescent carbon dots, as a promising fluorescent probe, exhibit many superior advantages compared with organic dyes and traditional semiconductor quantum dots, including good biocompatibility, low toxicity, excellent photostability, rich sources of inexpensive starting materials. These excellent properties endow CDs with great potential applications in bio-imaging, sensing, and photocatalysis fields, etc. [20–23]. Although several antibiotics sensing system using CDs as fluorescent probes have been developed [24], up to now, there is not any report on ENR detection based on CDs fluorescent probes.

Herein, fluorescent N-CDs were successfully prepared via a one-pot solid pyrolysis method, using DL-malic acid and glycine as carbon and nitrogen resources, respectively. The fluorescence of the as-prepared N-CDs was partly quenched in the existence of Cu2+ ions; afterwards the fluorescence of N-CDs-Cu2+ system was recovered after the addition of ENR. According to this principle, an ENR detection method based on N-CDs-Cu2+ system was constructed and employed to analysis of ENR in tap water and river water samples.

dl-Malic acid was obtained by Shenyang Dongxing Reagent Factory (Shenyang, China) and glycine from Beijing Biotopped Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). ENR, lomefloxacin hydrochloride and ciprofloxacin were purchased from Chengdu Xiya Reagent Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Chengdu, China). Copper sulfate pentahydrate (II) (CuSO4·5H2O) and other metal salts were obtained from Tianjin Standard Technology Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Britton-Robinson (B-R) buffer (pH 6.59) is a mixture solution of boric acid, phosphoric acid, and acetic acid, and each of them at 0.04 mol·L−1. The tap water sample was taken from our laboratory and river water sample from the north canal in Shenyang city (China). All chemicals were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification. Double-distilled deionized water was used throughout.

The morphology and size of N-CDs were characterized by JEM-100SX transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan) at an acceleration voltage of 100 kV. The samples for TEM observation were prepared by drying a drop of N-CDs aqueous solution dispersed on an amorphous carbon-coated copper grid. The crystal structure of N-CDs was determined using a Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer (Bruker AXS, Karlsruhe, Germany) using Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.1546 nm). The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a Nicolet Avatar 330 FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo Electron Corporation, USA) with a KBr pellet. The X-ray photoelectron spectra were recorded using an ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer (Thermo Fisher). UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained via a TU-1800PC UV–vis spectrophotometer (Varian Company, USA) equipped with a 1.0 cm quartz cell, while steady-state fluorescence measurements were conducted with a Cary Eclipse 300 FL spectrophotometer (Varian Company, USA) equipped with a thermostat bath and a 1.0 cm quartz cell. All the measurements were performed at room temperature if not specified otherwise.

In brief, 0.50 g of dl-malic acid and 0.50 g of glycine were mixed thoroughly in 50 mL of polytetrafluoroethylene autoclaves. Afterwards, the mixture was placed in a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 6 h. After the autoclave was cooled to room temperature naturally, 10 mL of water was added. For the sake of removing the unreacted precursors and other impurities, firstly the resulting brown solution was firstly filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane, and then purified through a centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min. At last, the supernatant was dialyzed through double-distilled deionized water in dialysis bag (molecular weight cutoff: 500 Da) for 48 h. The purified products were freeze-dried to get solid brown powder or dispersed into water to prepare the stock solution of N-CDs.

The quantum yield (QY) of N-CDs was obtained by referring to the previously reported literature [25,26]. Briefly, quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 medium was used as a standard (QY = 54%) and the QY of N-CDs was calculated by the following equation [27,28]:
Y =
Y R
I
I R
A R
A
n 2
n R 2
where Y is the QY, the subscript R refers to the corresponding parameters of standard, I is the integrated fluorescence intensity, A is the absorbance, and n is the refractive index of the solvent (n = 1.3325, nR= 1.336).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cu2+ quenching and ENR recovery procedure
ce:section.ce:para: In order to examine the effect of Cu2+ concentration on the fluorescence of N-CDs, 600 μg·mL−1 N-CDs and different amounts of Cu2+ were added into a 5.0 mL volumetric flask, and then diluted to the mark with B-R buffer solution (PH 6.59) to make final Cu2+ concentration of 10, 30, 50, 70, 100, 300, 500, 700, 1000, 1100 and 1300 μg·mL−1. The optimal excitation and emission wavelengths are 368 nm and 452 nm, respectively, which are used to measure the photoluminescence (PL) intensity of N-CDs-Cu2+ system for quantitative analysis.

In order to investigate the influence of ENR on the fluorescence recovery of the N-CDs-Cu2+ system, different amounts of ENR were sequentially added to a 5.0 mL volumetric flask containing 600 μg·mL−1 N-CDs solution and 500 μg·mL−1 Cu2+, afterwards, each mixture solution was diluted to the mark with B-R buffer solution (pH 6.59). After being fully mixed, the PL fluorescence of all the solutions was recorded at an excitation wavelength of 368 nm.

The detection of ENR was performed at room temperature. Typically, the desired amount of ENR was added into a 5.0 mL volumetric flask containing N-CDs (final concentration: 600 μg·mL−1) and Cu2+ (final concentration: 500 μg·mL−1) making final concentration from 0 to 15 μg·mL−1, and then the mixture solution was diluted to the mark with B-R buffer solution (PH 6.59). The fluorescence emission spectra were recorded after reaction for 4 min at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Collection and analysis of water samples
ce:section.ce:para: Tap water was taken from our laboratory and river water was sampled from the North Canal, Shenyang, China. All of the samples were filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane. And then those samples were adjusted to pH 6.59 with B-R buffer solution. The samples were stored at 4 °C prior to detection. Tap water or river samples were spiked with different volumes of ENR stock solution (final concentration: 2, 8, 13 μg·mL−1) and the spiked sample solutions were analyzed with the above described method.

Doped CDs can be used as versatile materials for future biomedical and sensing applications thanks to the unique fluorescent properties, excellent biocompatibility, and high aqueous stability [22,23]. At present, nitrogen-doped CDs were widely utilized for fluorescent detection of many molecules, such as pyridine, dopamine, amoxicillin, catechol and micro RNA [29]. Taking into account such a diverse range of doped CDs applications, this work aims to investigate the possibility for N-CDs based sensing system for detection of ENR.

The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the N-CDs was recorded and presented in Fig. 1A. There is a strong absorption peak located at 368 nm, which is attributed to the n – π* transition of CO bond [30]. The N-CDs aqueous solution can emit intense blue fluorescence under UV irradiation. Moreover, the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of the N-CDs were also scanned, respectively. From Fig. 1A, the maximum fluorescence intensity at λem = 452 nm can be observed at λex = 368 nm and the corresponding fluorescence Stokes shift of N-CDs was estimated to be 84 nm. The larger Stokes shift is more favorable for the analysis and detection in terms of fluorescence properties [31]. Finally, the QY of N-CDs was determined to be 20.5%, demonstrating that the as-prepared N-CDs have an excellent PL performance.

The typical fluorescence emission spectra of the N-CDs aqueous solution were shown in Fig. 1B using different excitation wavelengths varied from 348 nm to 418 nm with increments of 10 nm, demonstrating that the obtained N-CDs also exhibit an excitation wavelength dependent PL behavior, similar to the previous reports [20,21,23]. For further exploring the structure and morphology of the N-CDs, TEM (HRTEM), XRD, FTIR and XPS characterizations were carried out. The TEM image of the N-CDs shown in Fig. 1C clearly indicates that the N-CDs exhibit sphere-like shapes with an average diameter of around 2.56 nm and have a good dispersibility. According to TEM measurement, the size distribution of N-CDs is mainly in the range of 1.5–4.0 nm in diameter (Fig. 1D). The HRTEM image of N-CDs (Fig. 1C inset) shows the lattice fringes of N-CDs with a spacing of 0.21 nm, which matches well with the (100) plane of graphitic carbon [32]. Fig. 2A is the measured XRD pattern of the N-CDs, a typical wide and sharp peak around 2θ = 26.8° for (002) planes was observed, suggesting the existence of graphite-like structures [33], and indicating the crystalline properties of the N-CDs.

The FTIR spectrum of the N-CDs was recorded and depicted in Fig. 2B. The peak located at 3366 cm−1 was corresponds to the stretching vibration absorption of OH and NH group [34]. The peak located at 1659 cm−1 is the characteristic absorption peak of CO group. The peak located at 1400 cm−1 is related to the bending vibration of CH group and the peak located at 582 cm−1 should be ascribed to the bending vibration of NH group. FTIR results indicated that there were covered with a large number of hydrophilic groups, such as hydroxyl, carboxyl groups, and amine groups on the surfaces of N-CDs, which make N-CDs good solubility [35].

The XPS spectrum shows the existence of three elements, C, N and O with their contents 63.50%, 5.81% and 30.69%, respectively (Fig. 3A). The C1s band in Fig. 3B displays four peaks at t 284.3, 284.9, 285.8 and 288.1 eV, attributed to CC, CN, CO and CO, respectively [32]. The N1 s band (Fig. 3C) contains two peaks at 401.4 and 401.5 eV for CN and NH, respectively [20]. The O1s band (Fig. 3D) can be deconvoluted into three peaks of CO at 531.2 eV, CO at 531.9 eV and OH at 532.9 eV, respectively [31]. These results indicate that the N-CDs are rich in hydroxyl, carbonyl, and amino groups on their surfaces.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching and recovery of N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The mechanism for sensing system for ENR detection was depicted in Scheme 1 . As shown in Fig. 4A, the fluorescence intensity of the as-prepared N-CDs was dramatically declined after the addition of Cu2+, subsequently the PL intensity of the N-CDs-Cu2+ system was restored upon adding ENR. Therefore, both Cu2+ and ENR play fluorescence closing and opening roles in the whole sensing system, respectively.

In order to systematically investigate the quenching ability of Cu2+ on the N-CDs fluorescence, the fluorescence spectra of the N-CDs were scanned after adding different concentrations of Cu2+ ranging from 0 to 1300 μg·mL−1. It can be found from Fig. 4B, the fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs gradually decreased with gradual addition of Cu2+ along with a blue shift in the maximum wavelength. A plot of the concentration of Cu2+ against the fluorescence intensity ratio (F/F0) was drawn and depicted in the insert of Fig. 4B, where F0 and F are fluorescence intensities of N-CDs before and after adding Cu2+, respectively. The values of F/F0 shows a good linear relationship with [Cu2+] concentrations varying from 10 to 500 μg·mL−1 (R2 = 0.992) and a relatively poor linear relationship in the concentration range from 500 to 1300 μg·mL−1 (R2 = 0.939). Therefore, the final concentration of Cu2+ ions was chosen and kept at 500 μg·mL−1 in the further experiments.

As presented in Fig. 5A, the intensity and shape of N-CDs fluorescence did not change significantly after the addition of ENR, meaning that N-CDs did not interact with ENR. Therefore, it can be deduced that the fluorescence recovery of the N-CDs-Cu2+ system should be ascribed to the interaction between Cu2+ and ENR.

It can be seen from Fig. 5B that the scattering intensity of the N-CDs aqueous solution was obviously enhanced upon adding Cu2+, probably due to the formation of a larger size complex between Cu2+ and ENR [18] or the occurrence of the aggregation of some N-CDs [24], which can lead to a decrease in the intensity of N-CDs fluorescence (namely “turn off”).

Fig. 5C is the UV–vis absorption spectra of the N-CDs before and after adding Cu2+. Obviously, the intensity of the absorption peak around 368 nm was declined upon addition of Cu2+, which could be ascribed to the formation of a complex between N-CDs and Cu2+. Thus, Cu2+ ions may quench the fluorescence of N-CDs via the electron transfer from the photo-excited N-CDs to Cu2+ [15,19]. For this reason, the contribution of the N-CDs aggregation cannot be completely excluded in the quenching process of the N-CDs fluorescence caused by Cu2+. The FTIR spectra of ENR before and after adding Cu2+ were recorded and depicted in Fig. 5D. It is found that the strong peak located at 1736 cm−1 corresponding to CO group completely disappeared in the presence of Cu2+. According to the results of the above FTIR spectrum and the molecular structure of ENR, we propose the possible coordination mode of ENR and Cu2+ (shown in scheme 1). Thus, ENR should exhibit higher binding affinity toward Cu2+ than N-CDs when both of them bind to Cu2+. Hence, the addition of ENR can release some Cu2+ ions bound to the surfaces of N-CDs, consequently the fluorescence of N-CDs is restored (namely “turn on”).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of experimental conditions for ENR sensing
ce:section.ce:para: In order to obtain quantitative changes of the fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs-Cu2+ mixture solution with addition of ENR, the concentrations of Cu2+ and ENR were respectively fixed at 500 μg·mL−1 and 15 μg·mL−1, the effects of solution pH, concentration of N-CDs and incubation time on the enhanced PL intensity (ΔF = F/F0) were studied, respectively.

The pH value of buffer solutions is one of the important factors that influence fluorescence intensity. The plot of the ΔF as a function of pH in the range of 5.33–8.36 was drawn. As can be seen from Fig. 6A, the enhanced fluorescence intensity (ΔF) increased with pH values and reached a maximum value around pH 6.59, and then began to decline. The result of pH effect should be ascribed to the following two aspects: change of pH from acidity to basicity can result from protonation to deprotonation of N-CDs, which may further cause the change of the PL intensity [36]. In addition, various buffer solutions may have different effect, and with increase of the solution, Cu2+ may be partially precipitated. Thus, more N-CDs-Cu2+ complexes were dissociated along with recovery of fluorescence intensity of N-CDs. In our experiments, three kinds of buffer solutions (phosphate, B-R, and Tris-HCl buffer solutions) were used to control the pH of tested solutions, the results indicated that the B-R buffer solution gave the best result. Therefore, the B-R buffer solution with pH of 6.59 was selected in the following experiments.

The concentration of N-CDs is also related to the linear range and sensitivity of ENR detection, so it is necessary to study the effect of concentration of N-CDs on the ΔF. As shown in Fig. 6B, when the concentration of N-CDs changed from 15 μg·mL−1 to 1200 μg·mL−1, the ΔF increased firstly and reached a maximum at 600 μg·mL−1, then began to gradually decline. At higher concentrations of N-CDs (more than 600 μg·mL−1), the decrease in the ΔF should be due to the following reason. The increase of N-CDs concentration more than 600 μg·mL−1 did not significantly increased ΔF, because no Cu2+ ions were released from the surfaces of N-CDs with addition of ENR. Moreover, with the increase of N-CDs concentration, the occurrence of the aggregation of N-CDs resulted in the self-quenching of N-CDs fluorescence. Thus, 600 μg·mL−1 was chosen as the optimum concentration of N-CDs for the further experiments.

At room temperature, effect of incubation time on the sensing system was studied. As demonstrated in Fig. 6C, the enhanced fluorescence intensity (ΔF) is not quite stable when the incubation time is less than 4 min, and then ΔF kept nearly constant after 4 min. Hence, fluorescence intensity was measured at least 4 min after adding ENR in order to obtain a stable ΔF value for ENR sensing.

The fluorescence emission spectra of the N-CDs-Cu2+ solution in the presence of different concentrations of ENR were recorded under the optimal conditions. As demonstrated in Fig. 7 , the fluorescence intensity continuously enhanced with increasing amount of ENR. As presented in the inset (Fig. 7), there is a good linear relationship between the enhanced fluorescence intensity (ΔF) and ENR concentration ranged from 1.00 to 15.00 μg·mL−1 with a regression equation of ΔF = −4.72 + 3.07 [ENR] (μg·mL−1) and a correlation coefficient R 2 = 0.982. Whereas, beyond 15 μg·mL−1 for the ENR concentration, the above curve obviously deviated from linearity.

Moreover, the low detection limit (LOD) and low quantification limit (LOQ) for ENR were determined to be 0.16 μg·mL−1 and 0.53 μg·mL−1, respectively. The formula LOD = 3 σ/S and LOQ = 10 σ/S are used to estimate these two parameters, where σ and S represent standard deviation of the blank sample (11 replicates) and the slope of the calibration curve for ENR, respectively. The precision for this method was obtained by measuring 8 μg·mL−1 of ENR solution under the optimal conditions. The average result for 5 replicates was calculated to 8.20 μg·mL−1 with a relative standard deviation of 1.7%.

Usually, co-existing substances also may influence the detection of ENR in water samples, so the effects of potential interfering substances including Na+, Cl−, and K+ at 30 mmol·L−1; Mg2+ and Ca2+ at 10 mmol·L−1; Hg2+, Pb2+, Fe2+, and Fe3+ at 0.1 mmol·L−1; CO3 2−, HCO3−, SO4 2−, and NO3 − at 1 mmol·L−1; urea, glucose, and uric acid at 1 mmol·L−1; ascorbic acid at 0.5 mmol·L−1; LMFH (lomefloxacin hydrochloride) and ciprofloxacin at 0.01 mmol·L−1 on the fluorescence emission intensity of the N-CDs-Cu2+ system were evaluated (Fig. 8 ). After each interfering substance was added to the N-CDs-Cu2+ mixture solution containing 2.0 μg·mL−1 ENR, the fluorescence emission intensity of the mixture solution was recorded under the optimal conditions. The result showed that the relative error of the determination of ENR was within ±5%, indicating that these potential co-existing substances have little effects on the detection of ENR at these concentrations. Thus, the proposed sensing system exhibits an excellent selectivity for ENR detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of ENR in real water samples

ce:section.ce:para: In summary, highly fluorescent N-CDs were firstly prepared via a convenient and sample one-pot solid phase hydrolysis method using DL-malic acid and glycine as precursors. The N-CDs displays a strong blue fluorescence at 452 nm when excited at 368 nm. A novel analytical method of ENR was established by quenching N-CDs fluorescence with Cu2+ and then recovering the fluorescence of N-CDs by the addition of ENR. The constructed ENR sensing system exhibited good selectivity, wide detection range, and high sensitivity. Moreover, the sensing system was successfully employed to monitor ENR in real samples. This work provides a rapid, simple and accurate analysis method for detection of ENR in environmental water samples and also enriches the preparation methods and application scope of CDs.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledged partial financial support of Natural Science foundation of China (31371085) and China Association for Medical Education (2016SKT-M019).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2019.02.017.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Antibiotics in the aquatic environment–a review–part I
Chemosphere

Diam. Relat. Mater.

Lumin. J. Biol. Chem. Lumin.

Spectrochim. Acta A Mol. Biomol. Spectrosc.

Microchem. J.

J. Vet. Med. Sci.
A competitive chemiluminescence enzyme immunoassay for rapid and sensitive determination of enrofloxacin
Spectrochim. Acta A Mol. Biomol. Spectrosc.

Food Chem.

J. Pharm. Biomed. Anal.

TrAC Trends Anal. Chem.

Geoderma
